title: The bounce-back: surviving the blow-up hole
microFact: Everyone makes 9s. Good golfers make a 9 and then a par. The skill is the SECOND number.
lesson:
The round-wrecker isn't the triple bogey — it's the three angry holes AFTER the triple. Tilt is golf's real handicap.

Tools that work:

• THE 10-STEP RULE: you get ten angry steps off the green. Feel it fully, then it's over — next tee is a new hole. (Some players physically unvelcro and re-velcro their glove as the 'reset switch.')
• NO STORY-TELLING: "I always blow up on the back nine" is a script your brain will happily follow. The honest frame: one bad hole is ONE data point, already in the past.
• NEXT-SHOT FOCUS: the only shot that exists is the one in front of you. Not the 9 behind you, not the water two holes ahead.
• The stat that heals: even scratch golfers average worse-than-par rounds. Your net double bogey cap (byte 20) literally limits the damage — a blob is a blob, never a catastrophe.

Tournament truth: Stableford fields aren't won by the best golfer; they're won by whoever wasted the fewest holes on emotions. As a newcomer, equanimity after disaster is ALSO your loudest belonging signal — clubs are full of talented tantrums, and nobody wants to play with them twice.

quiz.question: You make a 10 on the 7th hole. The highest-value skill right now is…
options: Analyzing every mistake immediately while it's fresh
A short deliberate reset, then full focus on the 8th tee
Playing the next three holes extra aggressively to win it back
quiz.explanation: Brief reset ritual, close the file, next shot. Chasing losses and public flagellation both compound the damage.
